description: Having one thing means having it — or something else entirely.

guides.text: A new connective: $\lor$, pronounced 'or.' A **[disjunction|disjunction]** $p \lor q$ claims that at least one of $p$ or $q$ is true. The goal $A \to (A \lor B)$ says: if $A$ is true, then $A$ or $B$ is true. Adding an alternative can only make a statement **weaker**, never stronger.

To prove a disjunction, you don't need both sides — proving either one is enough. The rules are `or-intro-l` (put your truth on the left) and `or-intro-r` (on the right). An [implication|implication] goal still calls for our standard opening: assume the left-hand side.
We have $A$ at step 1. Use `or-intro-l` — the first argument is the step proving $A$, the second is the formula to put on the right ($B$). '$A$ is true, so $A$ or anything is also true.'
Now [discharge|discharge] the assumption. We proved $A \lor B$ by relying on $A$ — so $A \to (A \lor B)$ stands on its own.

unlocks.name: ∨-intro-l
unlocks.description: If you've proved $p$, you've proved $p \lor q$ — because the left side is already true. The $q$ can be anything.

unlocks.name: ∨-intro-r
unlocks.description: If you've proved $q$, you've proved $p \lor q$ — because the right side is already true. The $p$ can be anything.
